title: Episode 1

jokes.text: I opened a café with my friend Boo. Yeah, she's dead now.
jokes.setup: Fleabag begins what sounds like a warm, pleasant memory about opening a business with a friend
jokes.punchline: The brutal, breezy aside 'Yeah, she's dead now' delivered with zero ceremony, treating death as a minor footnote
jokes.explanation: An extraordinary cold open that does triple duty: it's funny, it establishes Fleabag's voice entirely, and it drops emotional devastation with the breezy casualness of a weather report. 'Yeah, she's dead now' is about as economical as comedy writing gets — four words that define an entire character's defense mechanism. The structure is near-perfect: the warmth of the setup against the flatness of the punchline. Only slightly below 9+ because the shock of this format has been used in other deadpan shows, but never this cleanly.

jokes.text: She's beautiful! [referring to Hilary, likely a guinea pig or animal, while mum is mentioned as dead]
jokes.setup: A family introduction moment; 'I suppose you should meet Hilary' sounds like a person is being introduced
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's delighted 'She's beautiful!' and the implication Hilary is an animal (likely a guinea pig) placed alongside the flat mention of the mother's death — the animal getting more warmth than the family tragedy
jokes.explanation: The joke is structural: introducing an animal with the formality of introducing a person, adjacent to a death announcement, reveals Fleabag's priorities and her slightly cracked emotional compass. On rewatch, knowing Hilary's significance to the series, this lands harder. The rapid emotional whiplash — death, animal, death — is characteristically Fleabag.

jokes.text: Mum died three years ago. Loo, doo, ooh, badoo!
jokes.setup: Another flat biographical fact: mother's death, stated plainly
jokes.punchline: The immediate nonsense scat 'Loo, doo, ooh, badoo!' — a verbal shrug, almost a childlike way of skipping past grief
jokes.explanation: Genuinely exceptional. The scat syllables as a grief-deflection mechanism is deeply character-specific and also structurally brilliant — it mimics exactly what people do when they can't process something, skip over it with noise. The tonal collision between death and nonsense babbling is jarring in a way that earns its laugh while making you slightly sick. This could ONLY be Fleabag. It justifies an 8.5 craft score by being original, economical, and deeply embedded in character psychology.

jokes.text: Dad's way of coping with two motherless daughters was to buy us tickets to feminist lectures, start fucking our Godmother and eventually stop calling.
jokes.setup: Dad as a caring figure trying to cope with grief — 'bought us tickets to feminist lectures' sounds almost touching
jokes.punchline: Escalation from mildly eccentric coping (feminist lectures) to 'start fucking our Godmother' to the bleakest endpoint: 'eventually stop calling' — each beat worse than the last
jokes.explanation: A masterclass in three-part escalation. The first beat (feminist lectures) is funny as an oblivious gesture. The second (fucking the Godmother) is a gut-punch surprise. The third (stop calling) is the bleakest possible landing — funnier than the explicit scandal because it's quiet abandonment. The word 'fucking' earns its keep here — casual vulgarity for a shocking fact. Highly quotable, deeply economical. Justified 8.5.

jokes.text: Jake's her step-son, he's really weird. Probably clinically but no one really talk about that.
jokes.setup: Introducing a peripheral family character as 'really weird'
jokes.punchline: The escalation to 'probably clinically' paired with 'no one really talk about that' — diagnosing someone while simultaneously dismissing the importance of diagnosis
jokes.explanation: A compact character sketch that also satirizes upper-middle-class British families' relationship to mental health — name it and ignore it simultaneously. The grammatical slip 'no one really talk about that' reads as nervous energy or Fleabag's slightly anarchic relationship to formality. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Your husband tried to kiss me on your birthday... He says it was more like the other way around.
jokes.setup: Fleabag trying to confess a serious accusation against her sister's husband at what should be a festive moment
jokes.punchline: Martin's counter-accusation flipping the narrative — a serious moment undercut by the absurdity of 'he said/she said' sexual politics delivered flatly
jokes.explanation: This is primarily dramatic but has a dark comedic layer — the instant flip from accusation to counter-accusation is played for ironic effect. Not strongly comedic but functions as cringe-comedy that implicates the audience in an uncomfortable truth. The laughs are uncomfortable rather than joyful.

jokes.text: Martin's being lovely. [Martin immediately identified as Always drunk]
jokes.setup: Claire describes Martin as 'being lovely' — a positive character endorsement.
jokes.punchline: Immediate counter-label 'Always drunk' — the descriptor collapses the endorsement instantly.
jokes.explanation: Clean economy of character introduction — we learn who Martin is through ironic juxtaposition. The 'lovely' / 'always drunk' rhythm is tight but not extraordinary.

jokes.text: This is a love story.
jokes.setup: We've just been introduced to a chaotic family dinner: grief, estrangement, betrayal, death — none of which reads as a love story
jokes.punchline: Fleabag directly addresses the camera with total earnestness to announce 'This is a love story' — a declaration that is functionally absurd given what we've seen
jokes.explanation: Four words that recontextualize everything before and promise everything after. The comedy comes from the irony — nothing we've seen resembles a love story — but the delivery must be sincere enough that it also functions as genuine genre announcement. This is a season-level tonal pivot disguised as a joke. Deeply quotable, structurally elegant, and only possible in a show that uses direct address. The economy score is a 10 because it achieves maximum comedic and dramatic impact with absolute minimum words.

jokes.text: Do you wanna have sex? No. Can I at least go down on you? No!
jokes.setup: Fleabag describing a moment of post-achievement clarity — 'you've done everything, you feel great, you're not even thinking about...' — building toward something transcendent
jokes.punchline: The transcendence she's describing is merely 'not asking someone for sex' — and she can't even hold that, because she did ask, and was rejected twice in rapid succession
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly constructed confession: the winding, almost meditative build-up ('you've done everything... you don't even think about...') parodies inspirational self-talk only to reveal Fleabag's 'achievement' is simply not propositioning someone — which she then immediately undercuts by revealing she did proposition them. The double rejection escalation ('No / NO!') lands as slapstick in verbal form. Character-specific because this compulsive self-sabotage is core Fleabag.

jokes.text: Congratulations, you assholes.
jokes.setup: A toast to love and family engagement, everyone celebrating warmly
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's private toast to camera: 'Congratulations, you assholes' — affection and contempt delivered simultaneously
jokes.explanation: Three words that capture everything about Fleabag's relationship with her family: genuine love expressed entirely through profanity and mild hostility. 'Assholes' here is a term of complicated endearment — she means it both ways simultaneously. Perfect economy. The fourth-wall delivery makes the audience complicit in her private verdict. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Dad's wedding toast devolving into extended verbal paralysis: 'So, uh, it means a great deal to both of us that, uh you... that we... the family, are... are... uh... are all... together here for, uh... For a very special family...'
jokes.setup: Dad stands to give what should be a touching speech at a family dinner celebrating his engagement
jokes.punchline: The speech degenerates into an extended ellipsis — half-formed thoughts, abandoned clauses, the verbal equivalent of buffering — culminating in '...gang bang'
jokes.explanation: The comedy here is structural torture — we sit in Dad's verbal collapse for a full 20 seconds of screen time, which makes it excruciating in the best cringe-comedy tradition. The extended hesitation is the joke; the 'gang bang' is the release valve. This works as a sustained comedic sequence rather than a single punchline, and scores well on structure because the slow build is deliberate and paid off.

jokes.text: May these be the worst of our days.
jokes.setup: Standard wedding/engagement toast territory — people are saying warm, optimistic things about the future
jokes.punchline: The Priest offers 'May these be the worst of our days' — an inversion of standard toasts ('may these be the best') that is simultaneously pessimistic, darkly funny, and oddly the most sincere thing said all evening
jokes.explanation: An exceptional joke and the Priest's defining introductory moment. Six words that invert toast convention while being genuinely profound — it expresses hope through pessimism, which is theologically interesting and also the funniest possible thing a priest could say at an engagement dinner. The joke works on three levels: as comedy (inversion), as character (this priest is unusual), and as emotional truth (we hope things get better from here). Justifies its high score by being original, structurally elegant, and deeply quotable — this line lives rent-free in viewers' heads.

jokes.text: Don't know who this guy is.
jokes.setup: A man at the party says 'Happy for you, old boy. Best decision a man can make' — a generic, confident toast
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's private aside to camera: 'Don't know who this guy is' — total social chaos underneath the veneer of family celebration
jokes.explanation: A small, clean reaction beat that does good work — the stranger confidently holding forth at a family gathering while nobody knows who he is captures the absurdity of the event perfectly. The deadpan camera-look is where the real laugh lives.

jokes.text: You look well, where've you been? Boots. It's lovely there this time of year.
jokes.setup: 'Where've you been?' is a social pleasantry expecting a holiday or interesting answer
jokes.punchline: 'Boots' (the UK pharmacy chain) — mundane to the point of comedy; the follow-up 'It's lovely there this time of year' treats a drugstore as a travel destination with straight-faced enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Beautifully economical. 'Boots' as a travel destination is funny on its own, but 'It's lovely there this time of year' — delivered with complete sincerity as a mock-travel endorsement — elevates it to genuinely sharp comedy. The joke punctures the social performance of small talk by refusing to play along. Highly quotable British absurdism.

jokes.text: Is that fur? Yes, but it's OK because it had a stroke.
jokes.setup: A guest notices what appears to be a fur item of clothing — a moment that sets up potential ethical discomfort
jokes.punchline: 'Yes, but it's OK because it had a stroke' — the animal's medical history offered as ethical clearance, which is both absurd and a perfect skewering of how the wealthy rationalize
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect joke. The logic is airtight within its own absurdist framework — dead animal = OK for fur — but the specific reason (stroke) is wonderfully arbitrary, as if the animal's cause of death is the relevant moral variable. It also lands as character comedy: this is exactly the self-justifying logic of someone who owns fur and doesn't want to think too hard about it. Highly quotable, original concept, clean structure. The 'Oh, lovely!' response from the other character adds a layer of shared social absurdity.

jokes.text: I can't go to hell for that, can I, Father? No. Not as long as you confess. Then you've nothing to fucking worry about!
jokes.setup: A casual aside to the Priest about the fur ethics — 'I can't go to hell for that, can I, Father?'
jokes.punchline: The Priest's cheerful 'Not as long as you confess' followed by Fleabag's commentary: 'Their cool, sweary priest. Love the Catholics! You can get away with anything. A lot of them did!'
jokes.explanation: 'A lot of them did!' is the sharpest element — a glancing reference to Catholic abuse scandals tucked into a jokey exchange about fur coats and confessionals. It's dark subversion disguised as a throwaway. The Priest's casual 'fucking' also establishes his character quickly. The joke chain works well as a unit.

jokes.text: Love the Catholics! You can get away with anything. A lot of them did!
jokes.setup: Observation that Catholicism's confessional structure means sins can be wiped clean
jokes.punchline: 'A lot of them did!' — the pivot from theological observation to oblique reference to institutional abuse, delivered as a casual aside
jokes.explanation: The joke hinges on 'them' being ambiguous for a split second — does she mean 'get away with things' generally, or specifically? The answer arrives immediately and darkly. It's brave comedy writing: making an institutional abuse joke at a dinner where a priest is present, as casual as commenting on the wine. The economy is excellent.

jokes.text: I didn't realise you were allowed out without your little doggy thing.
jokes.setup: Conversation with the Priest at a dinner party
jokes.punchline: The bizarre reference to a 'little doggy thing' — apparently meaning a clerical collar or some priestly accessory — expressed with genuine bafflement
jokes.explanation: A small, weird joke that works because of its specificity — 'little doggy thing' is such a strange description. The obliviousness of the speaker (treating clergy like exotic animals) is the real comedy. Solid but minor.

jokes.text: Needy waitress.
jokes.setup: A waitress asks if she can get the table another bottle before they've finished their current wine
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's two-word aside to camera: 'Needy waitress' — a tiny comedic editorial on the social dynamics of hospitality
jokes.explanation: Minor in isolation but gains rewatch value because the waitress becomes a recurring beat. 'Needy waitress' is Fleabag projecting — she runs a café and knows desperation when she sees it. The two-word structure is maximally economic.

jokes.text: Dream team. [Fleabag and Godmother both ordering identical sparkling water with lime]
jokes.setup: The Godmother and Fleabag, who clearly dislike each other, happen to order the exact same drink
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's withering 'Dream team' — maximum sarcasm for minimum occasion, the horror of having anything in common with someone you despise
jokes.explanation: Sharp and economical. The sarcasm is legible even without backstory, but lands harder if you know these two have specific reasons to loathe each other. The identical drink order is a tiny visual joke that Fleabag weaponizes with two words.

jokes.text: You know, the most fascinating thing about Father here is that his mother was originally a lesbi...
jokes.setup: A guest begins to share a genuinely interesting/personal detail about the Priest, mid-sentence
jokes.punchline: The sentence is cut off by a scene break — we never hear the end, the joke is the interruption itself, leaving the audience to imagine
jokes.explanation: The interrupted revelation is a classic comedic device but used with particular cruelty here — we're mid-word ('lesbi...'). The cut is the joke. The scene break withholds the payoff and the audience's imagination fills the gap with something funnier than any scripted conclusion. Rewatch bonus because you notice the structure more deliberately on repeat viewings.

jokes.text: Is there a reason that you're not drinking? He's an alcoholic. Oh, fun, my parents are alcoholics.
jokes.setup: Polite inquiry about why someone isn't drinking at a party — a socially sensitive moment
jokes.punchline: Claire bluntly outs her partner as an alcoholic; Fleabag cheerfully responds 'Oh, fun, my parents are alcoholics' as if this is something to bond over
jokes.explanation: 'Oh, fun!' as a response to someone's alcoholism — treating family dysfunction as a social icebreaker — is very Fleabag. The speed of the exchange and the casual 'fun' make it land. The Priest's parents' alcoholism also connects to his later revelation about them being 'very successful alcoholics.'

jokes.text: Oh, Claire! We thought you couldn't have them. What, why? Well, you just seem a little...
jokes.setup: Claire announces she and her husband are trying for a baby — a tender, hopeful moment
jokes.punchline: The Godmother immediately expresses shock ('We thought you couldn't have them') with no filter, then trails off on 'Well, you just seem a little...' — implying something deeply unflattering about Claire's maternal energy
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the Godmother's complete lack of social filter — the 'We thought' implies she's discussed this with others, making it worse. The trailing off ('Well, you just seem a little...') is funnier than any completion because it invites the audience to imagine what she means by 'a little.' Classic cringe-comedy construction.

jokes.text: Something's up. And now you have the money to pay for proper help. Ghastly without help, I imagine.
jokes.setup: Conversation about Claire's pregnancy attempt — a warm family moment
jokes.punchline: The Godmother's casual mention of 'proper help' (implying financial aid for fertility or childcare) followed by 'Ghastly without help, I imagine' — class-based condescension delivered as sympathy
jokes.explanation: A minor character joke that efficiently establishes the Godmother as someone who weaponizes wealth and class. 'Ghastly without help' is the key phrase — delivered as empathy while being fundamentally about status. Competent character comedy.

jokes.text: It caused quite a cultural wave. Ripple. Rip... w-wave. Wave.
jokes.setup: The Godmother is boasting about the cultural impact of her sex exhibition in Japan — building to 'cultural wave'
jokes.punchline: Dad quietly corrects her ('Ripple') — she tries to negotiate ('Rip...') then simply reasserts 'Wave' with complete authority, winning the argument through sheer refusal to back down
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly observed power dynamic: the Godmother correcting her own correction back to her original (wrong) word through sheer force of personality. 'Rip... w-wave. Wave.' captures the half-beat of someone almost conceding before doubling down. Dad's capitulation is implied and equally funny. This is sharp character comedy that reveals both their relationship and her inflated self-image.

jokes.text: No one's asked me a question in 45 minutes. So what do you do? Er, I run a café. Oh, wow! It's going well, is it? Yes, it is. It really is.
jokes.setup: Fleabag has been invisible at this dinner while others hold forth; the first question directed at her is the most generic possible
jokes.punchline: The question she's been waiting for is 'What do you do?' — the most perfunctory dinner party question — and her answer ('I run a café') gets the most tepid possible response ('Oh, wow!'). Then the internal beat where she has to sell her own success to herself.
jokes.explanation: The setup pays off the earlier detail that she runs a café — we're led to see how little this matters to anyone at the table. But the real comedy is in her self-affirmation: 'Yes, it is. It really is.' — the moment where Fleabag is convincing herself more than anyone else. The café's actual status is ambiguous, which adds weight to the desperation.

jokes.text: It actually is. It is. [internal reassurance to camera]
jokes.setup: Fleabag has just insisted the café is going well in company
jokes.punchline: Cut to her repeating it to camera: 'It actually is. It is.' — the compulsive need to convince herself, possibly lying, possibly genuinely trying to believe it
jokes.explanation: The fourth-wall break here is doing something more complex than comedy — it's making the audience complicit in Fleabag's self-deception. But it's also funny because the repetition ('It actually is. It is.') maps exactly onto the rhythm of someone who doesn't quite believe themselves. The comedy is in the gap between what she says and what the emphasis reveals.

jokes.text: Can I get anyone any ice? [the waitress, unprompted, returning with a new offer]
jokes.setup: The 'needy waitress' was already established; now she's back with an even more unnecessary offer
jokes.punchline: The escalation of neediness: from offering another bottle to offering just ice — the offer shrinks while the desperation presumably grows
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the needy waitress running gag — she's now offering ice, which is comedy through reduction. The joke is also structural: after a long scene of dinner party performances and one-upmanship, the waitress interrupts with the most pathetic possible offer. The gag is being run economically and earns its callback status.

jokes.text: So, do your family get together much, or...? Fuck you, then.
jokes.setup: The Priest asks a perfectly normal icebreaker question to Fleabag at what appears to be a smoking break
jokes.punchline: 'Fuck you, then' — Fleabag's response to an innocuous question implies the question inadvertently touched something real, and she responds with hostility that's both funny and revealing
jokes.explanation: Three words that do enormous work. We've just seen a 45-minute (within-story) dinner where family dysfunction has been on full display. 'Do your family get together much?' is the perfect unwitting intrusion. 'Fuck you, then' is Fleabag's entire relationship with her family distilled into a response — the comedy comes from the total disproportionality of the reaction to the question. It's also their first real interaction, which gives the eventual romance a foundation built on her telling him to fuck off. Rewatch bonus: this first exchange is perfect in retrospect.

jokes.text: Yeah, they can't even masturbate.
jokes.setup: The Priest is being discussed positively — 'He's such a lovely man' — then Dad asks about his religion ('they can't even...')
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's blunt completion: 'masturbate' — the one thing Dad couldn't say politely, stated by Fleabag with complete casualness, followed by 'Shit alive, man'
jokes.explanation: Fleabag finishing Dad's sentence with the word he couldn't say is efficient character comedy — it shows her irreverence and also her comfort with transgression. 'Shit alive, man' as the response (Dad? Another guest?) amplifies the absurdity. The joke is also setting up the ongoing thematic tension between Fleabag and the Priest's celibacy.

jokes.text: Did you always want to join the priesthood? Oh, fuck, no.
jokes.setup: A sincere question about vocation — expecting a considered, spiritual answer
jokes.punchline: 'Oh, fuck, no.' — immediate, emphatic, apparently involuntary — the priest's honesty shattering the expected piety
jokes.explanation: The Priest's complete disregard for the social expectation of a priestly answer — there's no hesitation, no pious framing, just 'Oh, fuck, no' — is excellent character establishment. The profanity from a priest remains funny but has been set up by his earlier 'fucking lonely.' The honest inversion of expected vocational mythology is sharp.

jokes.text: I came quite late to it, actually, but it's been a really good life to me. I've really found peace in it.
jokes.setup: Following 'Oh, fuck, no' — the Priest then delivers a sincere, calm account of his vocation
jokes.punchline: The immediate pivot from 'Oh, fuck, no' to genuine serenity is the joke — the whiplash between profane dismissal and authentic contentment
jokes.explanation: More dramatic than comedic, but the tonal whiplash from the previous line creates a comedic effect. Functions as character comedy — this priest is complex, not a stock character — but the laugh is minor.

jokes.text: Are your parents successful? They were very successful alcoholics, yeah.
jokes.setup: 'Are your parents successful?' — a perfectly ordinary question about professional achievement
jokes.punchline: 'They were very successful alcoholics, yeah' — redefining 'successful' as a metric applicable to alcoholism, with complete composure
jokes.explanation: An excellent joke. The word 'successful' applied to alcoholism — treating functional alcoholism as a measurable achievement — is a subtle linguistic inversion that gets funnier the more you consider it. The Priest delivers it as a straight answer, not as a joke, which makes it perfect deadpan. The word 'yeah' at the end grounds it as casual rather than performing wit. Highly quotable. Merits its 8+ on craft by being specific, structurally tight, and genuinely surprising.

jokes.text: Must be hard on the balls.
jokes.setup: Conversation about the Priest's vocation and celibacy — a topic being handled with some delicacy
jokes.punchline: Martin bluntly reduces the spiritual sacrifice of celibacy to its most physical component
jokes.explanation: Primarily valuable as character comedy — this is exactly what Martin would say, establishing him as the table's resident blundering vulgarian. Not particularly original, but fits the character. The setup for the Priest's comeback is the real payoff.

jokes.text: Not as hard on them as trying to make a baby for five months, I imagine.
jokes.setup: Martin makes a crude remark about celibacy being 'hard on the balls'
jokes.punchline: The Priest turns Martin's crudeness back on him with a precise, targeted burn about his own fertility struggle — destroying Martin with his own weapon
jokes.explanation: The Priest's comeback is exceptional because it uses Martin's own vulgarity as a weapon against him, while also revealing that the Priest has been paying close attention all evening. The precision of 'five months' shows he remembered the detail — it's not just a generic burn. The structure is classic comeback-with-escalation: Martin throws a grenade, the Priest returns it with a guided missile. Merits 8+ for structural elegance and the revelation of the Priest's character.

jokes.text: This sauce is disgusting. [immediately followed by] Oh, it's delicious, thank you.
jokes.setup: Someone at the table gives an honest private verdict on the food
jokes.punchline: The instant pivot to 'Oh, it's delicious, thank you' when the waitress arrives — pure social performance exposed
jokes.explanation: A minor comedic beat that observes British social hypocrisy efficiently. Not original — the 'say it's delicious to the cook/waiter' joke is a familiar territory — but executed with good timing through the transcript.

jokes.text: D'you know, I've always been so suspicious of religion but I must say I think there's something rather chic about having a real priest at a wedding. Are you a real priest? Yeah.
jokes.setup: The Godmother is performing sophistication — finding religion 'chic'
jokes.punchline: She undermines her own position by asking 'Are you a real priest?' as if she was expecting a decorative priest; his 'Yeah' is the flat confirmation that punctures her social performance
jokes.explanation: The Godmother's question 'Are you a real priest?' is the comedy — it reveals she's thought of him as an aesthetic accessory ('chic') rather than a person with an actual vocation. The Priest's bored 'Yeah' is perfect deadpan. Also: the Godmother's observation that religion is 'chic' is itself a joke about performative spirituality.

jokes.text: I'm new to the parish and I guess I'm just... I'm really fucking lonely! So... So I appreciate this. Thank you very much.
jokes.setup: The Priest explains why he's going out of his way to spend time with the wedding couple's family
jokes.punchline: 'I'm really fucking lonely!' — delivered with sudden emotional honesty and profanity in place of any social graces; then immediately pivoting to polite gratitude
jokes.explanation: This joke is structurally a cringe confession but it's also a character-defining moment — the Priest's loneliness is presented as both funny and immediately sympathetic. 'I'm really fucking lonely' is disarming because it's true and unguarded — a priest saying this in polite company violates all social expectations. The 'So... So I appreciate this. Thank you very much.' pivot back to manners after the outburst amplifies the comedy. On rewatch, knowing the season's arc, this is also the beginning of the love story. Justifies 8.0 for character integration and emotional economy.

jokes.text: What did he die of? Just, um, time.
jokes.setup: Question about the previous priest who died — expecting a cause of death (illness, accident)
jokes.punchline: 'Just, um, time' — death by old age presented as a mildly awkward thing to admit, as if it's embarrassing the previous priest simply ran out of it
jokes.explanation: 'Just, um, time' is a beautifully odd construction — the 'um' suggests the Priest finds this a slightly awkward answer, as if natural death requires apology or explanation. It reframes mortality as an embarrassing administrative fact. Highly quotable, economical, and the Priest's slight hesitation ('um') adds to the absurdist quality.

jokes.text: Father Patrick sadly died, so I got the gig.
jokes.setup: Explanation of how he came to the parish — expected to be a spiritual calling narrative.
jokes.punchline: 'I got the gig.' — Calling a priesthood assignment a 'gig' reduces it to the language of freelance entertainment. The casualness about a death + the showbiz vocabulary is the double joke.
jokes.explanation: 'The gig' is perfect — it treats the priesthood as a booking. The unselfconsciousness with which he says it (a man who just admitted being lonely) adds character depth. Strong economy.

jokes.text: Sounds like a riot. He was, actually.
jokes.setup: The Priest describes the previous priest as 'a dedicated man, a brilliant priest' — standard obituary praise
jokes.punchline: Fleabag sarcastically mutters 'Sounds like a riot' (meaning 'sounds boring'); the Priest confirms 'He was, actually' — literally accepting the metaphor as true
jokes.explanation: A gem of a two-line exchange. Fleabag uses 'riot' sarcastically (meaning 'boring'); the Priest takes it literally and confirms it as true, which inverts the irony. The confusion of registers — sarcasm answered with sincerity — is a classic comedic collision, but here it also functions as their first real moment of connection. The Priest gets her. The structure is tight: setup, sarcastic comment, sincere reversal. Merits 8.0 for originality of the inversion and the romantic setup it creates.

jokes.text: He plays the flute. The bassoon.
jokes.setup: The Godmother describes creepy step-son Jake as playing an instrument in the church band — 'just adorable!'
jokes.punchline: She says 'flute'; Dad quietly corrects 'bassoon' — a tiny detail wrong in a story being told with great enthusiasm, revealing she either doesn't know or doesn't care about the actual details of the children in her life
jokes.explanation: A miniature character joke — the Godmother's inaccuracy about a child's instrument while claiming an intimate story about him says everything about her relationship to truth and to others. Efficient and understated.

jokes.text: We don't want gifts at the wedding... So we've decided to ask people to make a small donation to a charity of their choosing, in our name.
jokes.setup: Standard wedding gift discussion — the Godmother announces a generous, selfless gift policy
jokes.punchline: 'in our name' — the charity donation isn't selfless at all, it's self-aggrandizing: she wants charitable acts done in her honor. The charity-as-vanity maneuver.
jokes.explanation: 'In our name' does the work — it's the tiny appendage that reveals the self-serving nature of the supposedly generous gesture. Classic satire of performative philanthropy, efficiently executed.

jokes.text: Do you want to know what gift I'm giving your father? A portrait. Of you girls. I'd only need a couple of sittings.
jokes.setup: The Godmother announces she has a personal gift for Dad — generating anticipation/dread
jokes.punchline: The gift is a portrait she will paint of the daughters — turning the daughters into a gift for their father, objectifying them while also imposing her own artistry on the relationship
jokes.explanation: The gift is uncomfortable because it removes all agency from both daughters — they become raw material for the Godmother's artistic gift to Dad. 'I'd only need a couple of sittings' treats them as subjects rather than people. The comedy is in the Godmother's complete blindness to how this lands.

jokes.text: No, because the lighting's never good enough and if you're not very photogenic then it does you no favours.
jokes.setup: Fleabag asks 'Can't you use photos?' — a reasonable alternative to painted sittings
jokes.punchline: The Godmother rejects photos partly because 'if you're not very photogenic then it does you no favours' — an implied insult to Fleabag's appearance disguised as practical advice
jokes.explanation: The implicit insult disguised as helpful explanation is classic passive-aggressive comedy. The Godmother manages to criticize Fleabag's appearance while appearing to offer a service. The deniability ('if you're not very photogenic' — not saying Fleabag isn't, but implying it) is the craft.

jokes.text: ...and the only photos there are of you two together are of when you were children and you looked like a boy.
jokes.setup: The Godmother continues her explanation of why she needs sittings, referencing existing photos
jokes.punchline: 'and you looked like a boy' — a completely unnecessary additional insult tucked into a practical explanation
jokes.explanation: The insult is gratuitous and therefore funnier — she didn't need to add this, she just couldn't help herself. The cheerful delivery of 'you looked like a boy' as a casual observation rather than a jab is peak Godmother cruelty.

jokes.text: You never told me you had a sister, Claire. Oh, well, we, um... We don't get to see each other much.
jokes.setup: The Priest innocently reveals Claire never mentioned Fleabag
jokes.punchline: Claire's awkward non-explanation: 'We don't get to see each other much' — a social dodge for 'we hate each other' that everyone at the table can probably read
jokes.explanation: A minor comedic beat in a largely dramatic sequence. The awkward evasion is readable, and the Priest having no idea this family is fractured is part of his ongoing obliviousness that makes him charming.

jokes.text: No, it's not that. Is he not in the Church? No, he's not in the Church. Oh, it must be so hard! Well, it's mainly hard... Is it cos he's Mummy's favourite? Because he's a paedophile.
jokes.setup: The Priest is being asked about his estrangement from his brother — guests guess various reasons (disapproves of career, not in Church, Mummy's favourite)
jokes.punchline: 'Because he's a paedophile' — delivered as a simple, factual answer, ending the guessing game absolutely
jokes.explanation: A masterfully constructed escalating-guesses joke. The comedy builds through each increasingly personal guess ('Is it cos he's Mummy's favourite?') only to be demolished by an answer that is simultaneously more serious and more absurdly delivered than anything anyone guessed. The flat delivery — 'Because he's a paedophile' as a conversational non-event — makes it shocking and funny simultaneously. The follow-up beat 'I'm aware of the irony of that' (Priest, Catholic Church, paedophilia) elevates it further. One of the episode's strongest jokes.

jokes.text: I'm aware of the irony of that.
jokes.setup: The Priest has just revealed his brother is a paedophile — a shocking statement in any context, but especially from a Catholic priest
jokes.punchline: 'I'm aware of the irony of that' — the Priest acknowledges the Catholic Church abuse scandal connection without saying it, doing so with complete composure
jokes.explanation: Among the best jokes in the episode and among the most daring jokes in the series. Six words that acknowledge institutional sexual abuse within the Catholic Church while being constructed as a personal statement about family irony. The Priest's calm self-awareness — he sees exactly what the audience sees — makes it funnier and more humanizing. This is what exceptional comedy writing looks like: it's specific, it's truthful, it's structurally perfect (the pause before it is implied and devastating), and it has the courage to go somewhere most writers would avoid. The 18-second gap in the transcript before the next line suggests a silence that is itself comedy. Merits 8.8 on craft.

jokes.text: Just a... just a breath of air, eh? [someone stepping outside after the paedophile revelation]
jokes.setup: The paedophile revelation has created an extraordinary silence
jokes.punchline: Someone breaks it with the most British possible response — stepping outside for 'just a breath of air' as if the problem is stuffiness rather than the conversation that just happened
jokes.explanation: A relief-valve joke after a big punchline — the understatement of needing 'a breath of air' after a paedophile confession is quintessentially British and effectively comic. Functions as a scene-reset.

jokes.text: It's just for you. [Dad gives Fleabag money privately, as a personal gift not for the café]
jokes.setup: Dad apologizes for missing Fleabag's birthday and gives her something, saying it's 'not for work'
jokes.punchline: The reveal 'It's just for you' — the implication being that Fleabag is used to gifts being contingent or conditional, or that personal money from Dad is rare enough to need clarification
jokes.explanation: More touching than funny — this is one of the episode's more genuine emotional moments. The comedy is in the awkwardness around the gift rather than in the gift itself. Minor comedic value.

jokes.text: You're not being naughty. No! Why?
jokes.setup: Dad and Fleabag are having an awkward reconciliatory moment — Dad notices something different about her
jokes.punchline: His observation 'You're not being naughty' as the specific, most telling thing he can say to her — and 'Why?' as Fleabag's instinctive, almost hurt response, as if good behavior is a problem requiring explanation
jokes.explanation: A deeply character-specific exchange. 'Naughty' is the word a parent uses for a child, which maps onto the arrested development in their relationship — and Fleabag's 'Why?' as a response to not being naughty is both funny and sad. She seems almost offended to have her bad behavior taken away. The comedy lives in the exact word choice and the 'Why?' is the punchline. Merits close to 8 on character integration.

jokes.text: I am so intrigued to see how you're going to make this whole evening about yourself.
jokes.setup: Fleabag and the Priest are having a private moment; he's clearly been watching her all evening
jokes.punchline: The Priest pre-emptively calls out Fleabag's self-involvement with a combination of amusement and affection — not a criticism but a delighted prediction
jokes.explanation: This is romantic comedy doing double duty — it's a genuine flirtation masquerading as a criticism. The Priest is essentially telling Fleabag 'I've been watching you all night and I find you fascinating' while framing it as mild disapproval. 'Intrigued' is the key word — it's not hostile, it's engaged. On rewatch, this is their courtship beginning. The line is highly quotable and lands with high impact because it reveals both characters simultaneously: the Priest's perceptiveness and Fleabag's performance. Merits 8.1 craft.

jokes.text: Oh, no. We probably shouldn't arrive at the table together.
jokes.setup: The Priest and Fleabag are having a private conversation outside — it's intimate but not improper
jokes.punchline: One of them (or both) realizes how it would look to return together — the implication of intimacy where there was only a conversation is funnier because they're both suddenly aware of it
jokes.explanation: A small but significant comedic beat: the awareness of appearances is itself the beginning of attraction. They haven't done anything and they're already managing appearances. The comedy comes from the gap between innocence and the performance of innocence.

jokes.text: I take all the negative emotions and just bottle them and bury them and they never come out. I've basically never been better.
jokes.setup: The conversation has turned to positivity and wellbeing — Fleabag appears to be joining in genuinely
jokes.punchline: 'I take all the negative emotions and just bottle them and bury them and they never come out' — a description of catastrophic psychological suppression delivered as a wellness endorsement
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most character-specific jokes. Fleabag describing her psychological damage as a wellness practice — 'bottle them and bury them and they never come out' said with complete earnestness as if this is the system working — is both funny and horrifying. This could only be Fleabag: the joke is that she's not joking. The irony is entirely structural; she believes what she's saying. The rewatch bonus is high because the entire season's arc is contained in this one line. Merits 8.1.

jokes.text: It takes real commitment to be this happy. It's not just about eating and drinking well, either. Putting pine nuts on your salad doesn't make you a grown-up. Fucking does.
jokes.setup: Conversation about the hard work of positivity — someone is building a case for what genuine wellbeing requires
jokes.punchline: 'Putting pine nuts on your salad doesn't make you a grown-up. Fucking does.' — the solemn declaration that the path to adulthood is sexual rather than dietary
jokes.explanation: The pine nuts line is excellent setup — it's the specific, snobbish detail of pine nuts (premium salad topping) as a symbol of attempted adulthood — and 'Fucking does' as the counter is both shocking and somehow logical in its directness. The rhythm is great: short declarative sentences building to the profane clincher. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: In Finland, we, um... they have this saying which I can't quite remember now.
jokes.setup: Claire begins to offer a profound Finnish wisdom to explain her happiness and wellbeing
jokes.punchline: She can't remember it — the 'wisdom' she was about to deploy doesn't exist, or she's simply forgotten, making the entire build-up collapse
jokes.explanation: The deflation of 'which I can't quite remember now' is the joke — all the cultural authority of Finland invoked, then immediately abandoned. It's also Claire's version of Fleabag's coping mechanisms: performative wellness that doesn't quite hold together under inspection.

jokes.text: And she is wide open lately.
jokes.setup: Someone describes Claire as having opened herself up to people who want to love her — a sincere sentiment
jokes.punchline: 'And she is wide open lately' — a statement that can be read as emotional (she's become vulnerable) or crudely physical, delivered with ambiguous intent
jokes.explanation: A decent double entendre that may be intentional (Martin) or oblivious (others). Functional but not particularly refined. The ambiguity is the best part.

jokes.text: What? No, she's a lawyer. I thought you were a lawyer? No. What? I work with lawyers, I'm not a lawyer.
jokes.setup: A guest assumes Claire is a lawyer; Martin confirms; Claire denies
jokes.punchline: The revelation that Martin doesn't actually know what his wife does for a living — he's been telling people for years she's a lawyer when she works in finance
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy — Martin not knowing Claire's profession is both funny and devastating. He's been telling people she's a lawyer. She went to business school. The confusion multiplies through the table, each character more bewildered than the last. It's a precision strike at their marriage's dysfunction through the most banal possible channel.

jokes.text: What's that in your hand? It's a voucher. For a counselling session. Thanks, Dad.
jokes.setup: Dad gave Fleabag a 'bedroom present' — something secret, presumably valuable or intimate
jokes.punchline: The big reveal: a counselling voucher — Dad's 'thoughtful' gift is essentially a referral to therapy, given privately so she wouldn't be embarrassed
jokes.explanation: The 'bedroom present' framing — meant to be opened privately because it's actually quite loaded — makes the counselling voucher land harder than it would as a straight reveal. 'Thanks, Dad' is Fleabag's flat acceptance, which reads as both sincere and ironic. The joke operates on the border of comedy and real tenderness.

jokes.text: That was meant to be a bedroom present. A what? A present that you open in your bedroom, alone.
jokes.setup: The counselling voucher has been revealed publicly; Dad explains it was meant to be private
jokes.punchline: 'A present that you open in your bedroom, alone' — the definition is both exactly right and somehow sounds deeply wrong in context, compounded by Dad's earnestness
jokes.explanation: 'A present that you open in your bedroom, alone' sounds mildly inappropriate when said aloud, which is the joke — it's entirely innocent but Dad has somehow constructed a phrase that sounds faintly salacious. The 'alone' is the detail that tips it.

jokes.text: I don't believe you can pay your problems away. I think you have to face who you are and suffer the consequences. It's the only road to happiness. Maybe happiness isn't in what you believe but who you believe.
jokes.setup: The dinner table has been discussing positivity, wellness, and the counselling voucher
jokes.punchline: The Priest's pivot to 'Maybe happiness isn't in what you believe but who you believe' — a statement that is technically about faith, delivered with such sudden sincerity that it lands as comedy in context, followed immediately by him excusing himself with 'Fuck. Excuse me.'
jokes.explanation: The philosophical statement landing at the dinner table like a depth charge, followed immediately by 'Fuck. Excuse me.' — as if the Priest surprised himself — is excellent tonal whiplash. On rewatch the line 'who you believe' resonates with the season's central romantic and spiritual question. The profanity-prayer collision remains his defining comic register.

jokes.text: Fuck. Excuse me.
jokes.setup: The Priest has just delivered a profound theological observation to the dinner table
jokes.punchline: He immediately excuses himself with 'Fuck. Excuse me.' — the profanity and the politeness arriving simultaneously, as if he's embarrassed himself by caring
jokes.explanation: Three words that distill the Priest's entire character: the profanity followed immediately by the social grace. He says 'Fuck' as a response to his own sincerity — he's embarrassed by having meant something in public. The 'Excuse me' then performs normality. This is the Priest's comic signature in its purest form. Economy score is 10 — maximum character revelation in minimum words.

jokes.text: Are you pissed off or are you doing a poo?
jokes.setup: Fleabag is outside a closed bathroom door; Claire has been in there a long time
jokes.punchline: The binary she offers: anger or bowel movement — a characteristically Fleabag reduction of emotional complexity to its most basic alternatives
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most Fleabag lines — the specific combination of emotional intelligence (she knows her sister is upset) and complete tonal disregard (the poo option). The binary forces Claire to declare either emotion or bodily function, leaving no room for the actual situation. It's also very funny just as a line reading. Merits 8.0 for character integration and economy.

jokes.text: Could try and fashion something with wings out of these. [offering hand towels]
jokes.setup: Claire needs a sanitary pad; there are only large hand towels available
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's practical suggestion to fashion a makeshift pad 'with wings' from the hand towels — the specificity of 'with wings' (a pad design feature) applied to a ridiculous DIY solution
jokes.explanation: The 'with wings' detail is what elevates this — it's such a specific product feature applied to an absurd DIY situation. Fleabag is problem-solving while also deflecting from what's clearly going wrong, and the humor comes from the incongruity of practical jokes in a crisis. The line also shows genuine sisterly care wrapped in comedy.

jokes.text: It's not a period, it's a fucking miscarriage, OK?
jokes.setup: Fleabag is treating the bathroom situation as a manageable inconvenience — trying to be practical and light
jokes.punchline: Claire's explosion: 'It's not a period, it's a fucking miscarriage, OK?' — the revelation that cuts through all of Fleabag's comedy with devastating force
jokes.explanation: This is structurally one of the episode's most important moments — it's the turn where the comedy gives way completely. But its effectiveness as drama relies on the comedy that preceded it (the hand-towel-with-wings joke, the needy waitress). The line itself is devastating and funny simultaneously: the 'fucking' applies to both the miscarriage (it's a terrible one) and to Claire's frustration at having to explain this. The structure (comedy → revelation) is the show's central technique executed perfectly.

jokes.text: Just get your hands off my miscarriage! It's mine.
jokes.setup: Fleabag is trying to help, possibly physically intervening
jokes.punchline: 'Just get your hands off my miscarriage! It's mine.' — the possessiveness of the statement is both heartbreaking and absurdly funny; the assertion of ownership over a loss
jokes.explanation: The episode's best joke by craft and impact. 'Get your hands off my miscarriage. It's mine.' achieves something almost impossible: it makes you laugh while devastating you. The possessiveness — 'It's mine' — expresses grief in the most unexpected possible form. Claire is asserting ownership over her own loss, refusing to let it be managed or mediated by anyone else. This is comedy that only emerges from character writing of the highest level. The absurdity (you can't physically take a miscarriage) collides with genuine grief (this is all she has left of the pregnancy). The economy is extraordinary — five words ('It's mine.') contain an entire emotional universe. Approaches the 9.5 craft anchor reference in the prompt. Deserves its high score.

jokes.text: Are you OK? Yeah, I'm, er... Er... Is, um... is it? No, I'm sorry, I just... Here we go.
jokes.setup: The sisters return to the table — Claire has had a miscarriage and they're trying to conceal it; Fleabag is clearly emotional
jokes.punchline: 'Here we go' — Fleabag's resigned aside to camera as the social machinery of concern fires up and she's about to have to manage the room
jokes.explanation: 'Here we go' is perfect Fleabag — the fourth-wall aside as emotional management, pre-empting the scene that's about to happen with weary acceptance. Small but well-placed.

jokes.text: I just had a little... I just had... I had a little miscarriage.
jokes.setup: The table is pushing Claire to reveal what's happened — 'No secrets here,' 'This is a safe space'
jokes.punchline: 'I had a little miscarriage' — the word 'little' is simultaneously a diminishment (Claire minimizing her own experience) and absurd (miscarriages don't come in sizes), both elements equally comedic and painful
jokes.explanation: 'A little miscarriage' — Claire's compulsive minimization of her own pain expressed through an impossible adjective. This is the most Claire thing she could say: she cannot allow herself to have big feelings publicly, so she shrinks a miscarriage to a 'little' one. The comedy and the tragedy are perfectly merged. The word 'little' does more character work than a page of dialogue. Merits 8.6 on craft: original, character-specific, structurally precise, deeply economical.

jokes.text: Whose was it? Maybe leave that for later.
jokes.setup: The table has just been told about the miscarriage; everyone is responding with concern
jokes.punchline: One person's immediate response is 'Whose was it?' — the most inappropriate possible question delivered with casual curiosity; Fleabag's 'Maybe leave that for later' is the comedy of an entirely reasonable response to an unreasonable moment
jokes.explanation: 'Whose was it?' in response to a miscarriage announcement is the wrong question in every conceivable way, and its wrongness is so complete that it becomes funny. Fleabag's diplomatic 'Maybe leave that for later' is the perfect straight-man response — acknowledging the absurdity without confirming it.

jokes.text: Was it the tooth man?
jokes.setup: Following 'whose was it?' — the table is attempting to identify the potential father of the miscarried pregnancy
jokes.punchline: 'Was it the tooth man?' — whoever 'the tooth man' is, the specificity of the nickname and its delivery as a serious investigative question is deeply absurd
jokes.explanation: 'The tooth man' — presumably a dentist, or someone known by this nickname — is both too specific and too absurd. The casual confidence of the question (he's a known quantity to this person) makes it funnier. The existence of someone called 'the tooth man' in Fleabag's orbit is a comedy gift.

jokes.text: No, I think I'll just deal with this in my own insane, irrational, anal way, if that's OK. / That's probably for the best.
jokes.setup: Claire is refusing hospital and everyone's help, doing it her way.
jokes.punchline: 'That's probably for the best.' — Dad agreeing that Claire managing a medical emergency in her 'insane, irrational, anal way' is probably fine. The agreement is both funny and a perfect portrait of their relationship.
jokes.explanation: Claire's self-description is funny ('insane, irrational, anal') and Dad's agreement is funnier — he not only accepts her characterization but adds 'probably for the best,' which is both a capitulation and a backhanded vote of confidence in her neurotic competence.

jokes.text: It's like a goldfish out the bowl, sort of thing. If it didn't wanna be in there, it didn't wanna be in there, something wasn't right.
jokes.setup: Claire has had a miscarriage; Martin is apparently trying to offer comfort or perspective
jokes.punchline: Martin describes the miscarriage as 'like a goldfish out the bowl' — a comparison of a lost pregnancy to a dead pet fish, delivered with the confidence of someone who thinks he's helping
jokes.explanation: Martin's miscarriage-goldfish analogy is one of the episode's great character reveals. The comparison is so catastrophically wrong — in concept, in delivery, in timing — that it defines him completely. The philosophy ('if it didn't wanna be in there, it didn't wanna be in there') applies the logic of pet refusal to a pregnancy loss. Martin thinks he's being consoling; every other person at the table presumably wants to kill him. Character integration score of 9 because this is Martin and only Martin.

jokes.text: Either way... she got her spotlight.
jokes.setup: Martin has already said the goldfish thing; now he doubles down with another perspective
jokes.punchline: 'She got her spotlight' — reducing Claire's miscarriage to an attention-seeking strategy, the most vile possible interpretation delivered as casual observation
jokes.explanation: The escalation from goldfish to 'she got her spotlight' is extraordinary. Martin has now moved from a bad analogy to actively blaming Claire for having a miscarriage to get attention. The casual cruelty — 'either way' implies both options (miscarriage or not) were equally manipulative — is so appalling that it becomes darkly comic. This line is what gets Martin hit, which is the correct narrative consequence. The build from bad to monstrous across two lines is perfect escalation writing. Merits 8.6 on craft.

jokes.text: Whoa, whoa, whoa, whoa! What the fuck? Fuck! Oh, fuck! Jeez... Jesus Chr...! Is there anything I can do?
jokes.setup: Martin has just said 'she got her spotlight' — the most offensive thing said all evening
jokes.punchline: What appears to be a physical altercation breaking out, followed by someone (possibly the Priest) asking 'Is there anything I can do?' — the most understated possible response to chaos
jokes.explanation: The fight itself is primarily visual comedy — in the transcript we only hear the chaos. The calm 'Is there anything I can do?' landing in the middle of the chaos is the verbal comedy, possibly the Priest again demonstrating his understated response to escalating situations. High impact because it's the release valve after a long build of tension.

jokes.text: Can I do anything? No, thank you. They've gone. So...
jokes.setup: The episode opened with this exact exchange — 'Can I do anything? No, thank you. They've gone. So...'
jokes.punchline: The callback to the opening — we've now seen what 'They've gone' refers to, and 'So...' carries entirely different weight having seen the evening's events
jokes.explanation: This is a structural joke as much as a comedic one — the episode has looped back to its beginning and we now understand the context of the opening scene. 'They've gone. So...' is now devastating because we know who 'they' are and what kind of evening it's been. The 'So...' trails off into the same void as before. On rewatch (or even first viewing if you noticed), this is deeply satisfying construction. Merits 8.5 for structural elegance.

tmdb_overview: Fleabag celebrates Godmother and Dad’s engagement and is intrigued by their new Priest, but the evening comes to stormy end when old tensions resurface.
